title: Chapter 23: The Silence of Green Leaves
paragraphs: When the darkness dissipates, leaving behind only ash, time loses its meaning. The great battle at the Gates of Etcha ended not with a victory cry, but with an absolute, ringing void. The Abomination crumbled into gray dust, but none of the survivors looked at the defeated enemy.
All eyes were fixed on the edge of the smoking crater.

Avotu, the last apprentice of Sivarus, slowly rose to his feet. His armor was covered in dents, his breath escaped his chest with a whistle, but the physical pain was insignificant compared to what was now happening in his mind.
He walked toward the edge of the crater with a staggering gait. There, on the charred, acid-burned stone, lay what was left of Yashil.

The small goblin's body was hopelessly broken. The fragile bones could not withstand even the residual shockwave. His chest was unnaturally caved in, and from under the torn burlap, thick green blood seeped in steady pulses, soaking the gray ash.

Avotu sank heavily to his knees. The elf, who for millennia had carried the sorrow of battles on his shoulders, looked at a being whom the world considered filth. A goblin with no magic, no mastery of the sword, had just pushed him from death. He had given his only, tiny life for one who himself sought destruction.

The black flame on Avotu's head flickered. It still absorbed light—but differently. Not as greedily. As if somewhere in its impenetrable depth, a single ember had appeared that simply stopped going out.
Behind him came a sound that made blood run cold. It was the sound of a breaking soul.

Kirah, the giant demon of destruction, crawled through the jade debris, dragging his torn paws. He stopped next to Yashil. The huge resinous creature was afraid even to touch the goblin's broken body, lest he cause him even more pain. Huge, impossible tears rolled from the demon's burning ruby eyes, hissing and evaporating on the cold stones.

"Teacher..." Kirah rasped. His voice trembled so much that the words could barely be made out. "Why... why did you step forward? You should have hidden behind my back. I was created for this. Not you."

Rene approached next. The fox moved slowly, as if each step cost her unbearable effort. Her posture was broken. Her yellow eyes, which had always looked at the world with cynical boredom, were now wide open and full of tears that smeared the cherry paint on her lips. She knelt right in the mud and carefully, with the tips of her fingers, touched Yashil's cooling green hand.
"You are a terrible egoist," Rene whispered, her voice breaking into a sob. "You made us believe there was meaning in this world... and then you leave."

Dzhanavar walked silently to the crater. The werewolf did not utter any quotes. There was not a drop of his former mock-bravery in his wolf muzzle. The giant predator drove his double-edged axe deep into the stone at Yashil's head as if erecting a headstone, and bowed his head heavily, closing his eyes.

Lord Valthor stood at a distance. The vampire looked at the scene, and there was no familiar centuries-old indifference in his eyes. He waved his hand, and the legion of the dead that had saved the refugees silently disintegrated into dust, returning to the ground. The immortal lord slowly, with respect, bowed his head to the mortal goblin.
Yashil struggled to open his eyes. His breathing was shallow, with a light, wet bubbling. He tried to focus his gaze on the faces of those leaning over him.
The goblin's lips twitched slightly in an attempt to smile.

"Old Gryz..." Yashil's voice was quieter than the rustling of falling ash, but in the ringing silence, every sound echoed like thunder. "Old Gryz said... giving is the lot of fools. In this world... only the one who takes survives."
The goblin gasped for air, green blood bubbling on his lips. Avotu carefully slid his plate gauntlet under his head, lifting it.

"My mother..." Yashil continued, looking through the leaden clouds above Etcha as if he saw the clean, clear stars of a distant past beyond them. "My mother gave everything. She wasn't a fool, Kirah. She was... the strongest."
He turned his fading gaze to the huge demon.
"Don't let your darkness... decide who you are, my friend. You are good, Kirah. Warm those who are cold."
Then Yashil looked at Rene and Dzhanavar.
"Protect each other. You... are the best pack."
Finally, his dark, wise eyes met Avotu's.

"You promised to be a shield, traveler," the goblin whispered. "Your pain... it's just your love that has nowhere to go. Give it to those who are still alive. And then... no one will vanish into the darkness forever."
With a heavy, grinding sound, a small leaf of Etcha's Great Gates opened slightly.

From there, stumbling and dropping weapons, came elven guards. They were led by the same captain who had once struck Yashil in the face. They had stood on the balcony. They had seen everything. They had seen the High Elves close the doors in the face of death, and they saw a tiny goblin step out to meet it.

The captain slowly approached the edge of the crater. His golden armor, a symbol of centuries of superiority, now seemed a heavy, suffocating cage. The elf looked at the green blood mixed with ash. He looked at the demon mourning the goblin and at the great warrior holding him in his arms.
The centuries-old wall of elven pride, racial hatred, and blind arrogance collapsed.

The captain unbuckled his baldric. His sword fell to the stones with a clang. The elf slowly knelt before Yashil. One by one, throwing aside helmets and dropping halberds, all the guards of Etcha knelt. The crowd of saved refugees—dwarves, half-orcs—followed their example.

Hundreds of beings of different races bowed their heads in absolute, reverent silence. Not before magic. Not before strength. But before what this little goblin from the Rotten Hollow most wanted to prove to them.
Yashil saw this. His eyes filled with quiet, all-encompassing peace.
"Mama..." Yashil exhaled barely audibly. "Look... they aren't afraid of us anymore."
The light in the goblin's dark eyes gently went out without suffering. His chest slowly lowered one last time and did not rise again.
The wind over the wastelands died down completely.

Avotu carefully, with infinite tenderness, lowered Yashil's head to the stone. The black flame above the elf's head did not change color. It was as dark as ever. Но something in it had become almost imperceptibly different—as if a flame that had burned in the void for a long time now had a reason not to go out. Avotu took the goblin's words into the very depths of his tortured heart. He did not yet know what to do with them. Но he carried them.

In a fading world, where every day was permeated with the selfishness of survival, a single life turned everything over. And even though Yashil of the Rotten Hollow closed his eyes forever, his light could no longer be extinguished.
